title: Log-concavity of Polynomials Arising from Equivariant Cohomology
speaker: Yairon Cid-Ruiz
abstract:
A remarkable result of Brändén and Huh tells us that volume polynomials of projective varieties are Lorentzian polynomials. The dual notion of covolume polynomials was introduced by Aluffi by considering the cohomology classes of subvarieties of a product of projective spaces. 
In this talk, we shall address the equivariant cohomology classes of torus-equivariant subvarieties of the space of matrices.
For a large class of torus actions, we shall show that the polynomials representing these classes (up to suitably changing signs) are covolume polynomials in the sense of Aluffi. If time permits, we shall present a description of the cohomology rings of smooth complex varieties in terms of a generic Macaulay inverse system over the integers.
This is based on joint work with Yupeng Li and Jacob Matherne.

proof_stages.label: Multidegrees and known log-concavity theorems
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines multidegrees of biprojective or multiprojective schemes and reviews the progression from positivity/no-intermediate-zero results to log-concavity and Lorentzian volume-polynomial theorems for irreducible varieties.

proof_stages.label: Support questions beyond irreducibility
proof_stages.summary: Motivated by the irreducible case, the speaker formulates the problem of understanding supports of cohomology classes for reducible or ACM varieties, including a conjectural saturated-Newton-polytope behavior for ACM schemes.

proof_stages.label: Covolume polynomials as the target positivity class
proof_stages.summary: Aluffi's covolume polynomials are introduced as limits of multiprojective cohomology-class polynomials, and their relation to Lorentzian normalized complements explains why proving covolume-ness yields log-concavity-type consequences.

proof_stages.label: Equivariant matrix-space theorem
proof_stages.summary: For a torus-invariant subvariety of the affine space of matrices, the main theorem states that the representative of its torus-equivariant cohomology class becomes a covolume polynomial after changing the signs of the negative-weight variables.

proof_stages.label: Translation to multidegrees of positively graded prime ideals
proof_stages.summary: Using the Knutson-Miller description of equivariant classes by K-polynomials and multidegrees, the theorem is reduced to proving that the multidegree of any prime ideal with a positive grading is covolume.

proof_stages.label: Standardization of nonstandard gradings
proof_stages.summary: The key construction replaces each nonstandardly graded variable by a product of standardly graded variables, producing an ideal in a standard multigraded ring with the same Betti numbers, K-polynomial, and multidegree, while preserving primeness after handling variables.

proof_stages.label: Multiprojective realization and covolume conclusion
proof_stages.summary: After standardization, the same multidegree is realized in a product of projective spaces, so it is a covolume polynomial; this completes the proof of the equivariant theorem and explains its double-polynomial applications.

proof_stages.label: Independent addendum on generic Macaulay inverse systems
proof_stages.summary: In a brief separate part, the speaker recalls Artinian Gorenstein algebras and Macaulay dual generators, then describes a relative or generic inverse-system framework for finite graded algebras over a base.

examples.engagement_reason: The speaker describes mixed multiplicities as known bidegrees with a positivity range and an open question, but does not compute or verify them for an ideal.

examples.name: A non-split torus action on affine two-space
examples.note: The origin in a two-dimensional torus representation with mixed positive and negative weights has equivariant class t1^2 - t2^2, illustrating why the positive/negative weight separation hypothesis is needed.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker writes an explicit torus action on C^2, takes the origin, computes its class as (t1+t2)(t1-t2)=t1^2-t2^2, and checks the support failure.

passes.reason: An ordinary torus representation with mixed-sign weights used to show arbitrary torus actions need not give SNP or convex support.

examples.name: Matrix Schubert varieties and double Schubert polynomials
examples.note: Matrix Schubert varieties are presented as a standard application: their equivariant classes are double Schubert polynomials, so the main theorem implies the signed double Schubert polynomial is covolume.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the main theorem to matrix Schubert varieties via the known identification of their equivariant classes with double Schubert polynomials to derive the covolume corollary.
